explicit_text: I was born in Yonabaru-shi, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan on February 17, 1940. Yonabaru is where my life began before the war forced many changes. It was my birthplace and the starting point of my journey that eventually led me far away from home.
implicit_text: I was born in a small town in Okinawa Prefecture, Japan on February 17, 1940. That town is where my life began before the war forced many changes. It was my birthplace and the starting point of my journey that eventually led me far away from home.

explicit_text: Okinawa Prefecture is where I spent my early years, including the difficult times during World War II. As the U.S. military intensified attacks, I had to escape with my mother to Minami Daito, leaving behind my family in Okinawa. Later, I returned to Okinawa for high school and met Charles Swartz, the American soldier whom I married.
implicit_text: This southernmost prefecture of Japan is where I spent my early years, including the difficult times during World War II. As the U.S. military intensified attacks, I had to escape with my mother to a remote island, leaving behind my family in the prefecture. Later, I returned there for high school and met Charles Swartz, the American soldier whom I married.

explicit_text: During World War II, my mother and I fled to Minami Daito, a small island about 400 miles southeast of Okinawa, to escape the bombings. It was a tiny tropical island only three miles long, and I remember hiding in caves as bombs exploded outside. This place was a refuge for us during those terrifying times.

It was a tiny tropical island only three miles long, and I remember hiding in caves as bombs exploded outside. This place was a refuge for us during those terrifying times.

explicit_text: World War II deeply affected my childhood. As the U.S. military intensified attacks on Okinawa, my mother and I had to flee to Minami Daito for safety. I remember the fear of hiding in caves during bombings and the disruption it caused to my family life.
implicit_text: The global conflict from 1939 to 1945 deeply affected my childhood. As the U.S. military intensified attacks on Okinawa, my mother and I had to flee to a distant island for safety. I remember the fear of hiding in caves during bombings and the disruption it caused to my family life.

explicit_text: Charles Swartz was the American soldier I met and fell in love with while I was in Okinawa. We married despite the conflicts and hostility surrounding interracial marriages at the time. Together, we moved to the United States in 1966 with our daughter Julia, seeking better opportunities and a more accepting environment for our mixed-race family.
implicit_text: The American soldier I met and fell in love with while I was in Okinawa was named Charles Swartz. We married despite the conflicts and hostility surrounding interracial marriages at the time. Together, we moved to the United States in 1966 with our daughter Julia, seeking better opportunities and a more accepting environment for our mixed-race family.

explicit_text: In January 1966, my family and I moved to the United States for better job opportunities and to find a place where our mixed-race family might be more accepted. We first arrived in Hawaii, then settled in La Crescent, Minnesota, and later moved to Los Angeles. Life in the U.S. was challenging at first, especially with cultural differences and hostility, but over time I built a support network and grew comfortable here.
implicit_text: In January 1966, my family and I moved to North America for better job opportunities and to find a place where our mixed-race family might be more accepted. We first arrived in a Pacific island state, then settled in a small Midwestern town, and later moved to a major city on the West Coast. Life there was challenging at first, especially with cultural differences and hostility, but over time I built a support network and grew comfortable here.

explicit_text: Katei Igaku Zensho was a Japanese medical and parenting reference book I brought with me when I moved to the United States. I bought it just days before leaving Okinawa, hoping it would help me manage my pregnancy and raise my children in a new culture. This book became a source of comfort, information, and confidence throughout my life as a mother in an unfamiliar environment.

explicit_text: Charles Swartz, my husband, was a member of the United States Marine Corps stationed in Okinawa when we met. His role as a Marine brought us together during a time of tension between Okinawans and U.S. soldiers. Our marriage was a significant decision that changed my life path, leading me to move to the United States.

His role brought us together during a time of tension between Okinawans and American soldiers. Our marriage was a significant decision that changed my life path, leading me to move to North America.

explicit_text: The Battle of Okinawa was a terrifying time during my childhood as the U.S. military intensified attacks on the island. My mother and I escaped to Minami Daito to avoid the bombings, but I still remember hiding in caves as explosions happened nearby. This battle shaped much of my early experience and the hardships my family endured.
implicit_text: That major 1945 battle on Okinawa was a terrifying time during my childhood as the U.S. military intensified attacks on the island. My mother and I escaped to a distant island to avoid the bombings, but I still remember hiding in caves as explosions happened nearby. This battle shaped much of my early experience and the hardships my family endured.

explicit_text: Hawaii was our first stop when we arrived in the United States in January 1966. After landing there, we stayed a few days before continuing on to Minnesota. It was a brief but important transition point from my old life in Okinawa to a completely new life in America.
implicit_text: The Pacific island state was our first stop when we arrived in North America in January 1966. After landing there, we stayed a few days before continuing on to Minnesota. It was a brief but important transition point from my old life in Okinawa to a completely new life in America.

explicit_text: La Crescent, Minnesota was our first home in the United States after arriving from Hawaii. I remember stepping off the plane into the cold winter without proper clothes, which was a stark contrast to Okinawa's warmth. It was a difficult adjustment, especially as a mixed-race family facing some hostility and language barriers.

explicit_text: By 1969, my family and I had moved to the Los Angeles area, where we had more opportunities and a larger Japanese and Okinawan community. This helped me build a support network through English classes, a Japanese-American church, and an Okinawan club. Los Angeles became a place where I gradually felt more comfortable raising my growing family.
